chunk_summary: This section outlines first-hour anaphylaxis management, emphasizing immediate epinephrine administration, ABC assessment, and observation for biphasic reactions, with discharge planning and caregiver education.
learning_objectives: Understand the immediate steps for managing suspected anaphylaxis, including epinephrine dosing and ABC assessment.
Recognize the importance of observation for biphasic reactions and discharge planning with caregiver education.
Differentiate between anaphylaxis and FPIES to avoid inappropriate treatment.
Learn the role of adjunct therapies and their timing in anaphylaxis management.
key_facts: Administer intramuscular epinephrine at 0.01 mg/kg per dose (1 mg/mL, 1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh, repeating every 5–10 minutes if symptoms persist.
Biphasic reactions occur in 1–20% of cases, often within 10 hours but up to 72 hours post-recovery.
Position the child supine or sitting (if vomiting/respiratory distress) and provide high-flow oxygen as indicated.
In refractory shock, escalate to IV epinephrine and vasopressors after isotonic fluid bolus (20 mL/kg).
FPIES is not IgE-mediated; focus on hydration and antiemesis rather than epinephrine in such cases.
reference_quote: India-adapted guidance recommends positioning the child supine (or sitting if vomiting/respiratory distress), rapid ABC assessment, high-flow oxygen when indicated, and prompt intramuscular epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg per dose (1 mg/mL, '1:1000') into the anterolateral thigh.
document_key_topics: Differentiation of FPIES from IgE-mediated anaphylaxis
